description: Length-matched context and memory limitation facts for LLMs.

facts.context: The 'lost in the middle' phenomenon refers to:
facts.truth: LLMs attending poorly to information placed in the middle of long contexts
distractors: uniform attention distribution across all positions in the context window
degraded performance affecting only the very start of the input sequence
a problem fully solved by simply increasing the model's context window size

facts.context: Extending context windows via position interpolation has the limitation that:
facts.truth: effective utilization of extended context degrades even if technically supported
distractors: position interpolation enables perfect utilization of all extended positions
longer context windows always proportionally improve model output performance
no quality degradation occurs when extending context beyond training length

facts.context: KV cache memory requirements for long contexts grow:
facts.truth: linearly with sequence length, becoming a major deployment bottleneck
distractors: remaining constant regardless of the input or generated sequence length
logarithmically with sequence length making long contexts very efficient
negligibly compared to static model weight memory during inference time

facts.context: LLMs are fundamentally stateless between API calls, meaning:
facts.truth: all context must be re-provided each call with no persistent memory
distractors: models retain and recall all information from every past conversation
conversational state persists automatically across separate API sessions
memory between calls is unlimited and requires no external infrastructure

facts.context: Episodic memory (remembering specific past interactions) in LLMs requires:
facts.truth: external storage and retrieval since parametric memory cannot be updated
distractors: built-in episodic memory modules that are standard in transformer design
no additional infrastructure beyond the existing model weight parameters
real-time weight updates that modify parameters during inference directly

facts.context: Effective working memory in transformers is limited by:
facts.truth: attention capacity and ability to use context positions strategically
distractors: only the total number of transformer layers in the model architecture
vocabulary size being the sole bottleneck on working memory capacity
nothing at all since transformer working memory is effectively unlimited

facts.truth: ability to retrieve specific information placed anywhere in long context
distractors: only open-ended generation quality without regard to retrieval accuracy
whether model has memorized specific training data documents verbatim
exclusively how model parameter count affects performance on basic tasks

facts.context: Adding recurrence or state to transformers (like Mamba, RWKV) trades off:
facts.truth: constant memory for potentially reduced ability to access arbitrary past tokens
distractors: nothing at all since recurrence is strictly superior to pure attention models
only training speed for no benefit to final model quality or capability set
only training compute efficiency without affecting inference-time performance

facts.truth: losing fine-grained details that may be critical for downstream tasks
distractors: no information loss since compression preserves all semantically relevant content
always improving output quality by removing noise from the input context window
automatically preserving all important details through learned selection criteria

facts.context: Updating factual knowledge in LLMs without full retraining is difficult because:
facts.truth: knowledge is distributed across many parameters without localized storage
distractors: individual parameters each store exactly one fact enabling surgical editing
knowledge editing techniques are reliable and fully production-ready today
factual knowledge updates require zero additional computational resources
